summary: Jeff Atwood explores Eric Sink's framework for categorizing software based on who uses it: MeWare (developer only), ThemWare (users only), and UsWare (both developer and users). He argues that ThemWare—where developers build for users they don't understand—produces predictably poor results because developers can't empathize with user needs. The best software emerges when developers are also users, allowing them to naturally understand what needs improvement. Atwood advocates for 'dogfooding' as a practice to bridge the gap between developers and users, even when it's challenging.
key_points: Software quality correlates strongly with whether developers actually use what they build
ThemWare developers see features in terms of implementation difficulty rather than user value
Becoming a user—even 'in spirit'—transforms mediocre ThemWare into superior UsWare
Dogfooding keeps developers honest and aligned with user needs
The goal should be working alongside users rather than against them
money_quotes: If I am building software that I don't use and don't know how to use for people I don't understand or even like, how good is my software going to be?
I probably see every feature in terms of how difficult it will be to implement, rather than how valuable it will be for my users.
Much of the best software is the best because the programmers are the users, too. It is UsWare.
To really care about the software you're writing, you have to become a user, at least in spirit.
Why work against your users by producing ThemWare when you could work alongside them to build UsWare?
key_insight: The quality gap between mediocre and great software often comes down to whether developers experience their own product as users do.
